title: The Interview by Christopher Silvester / Umberto Eco
subject: English

notes.heading: Theme
notes.text: The chapter explores the value and danger of interviews, the ethics of public questioning, and Umberto Eco’s discipline in using small moments of time.

summary:
Part I, written by Christopher Silvester, explores the history and controversy of interviews. Though interviews are only about 130 years old, they have become a routine part of journalism. Many famous personalities have disliked them. V. S. Naipaul says people are wounded by interviews and lose a part of themselves. Lewis Carroll avoided interviews throughout his life. Rudyard Kipling called the interview immoral, a crime, an assault, and a cowardly act. H. G. Wells described interviewing as an ordeal, and Saul Bellow compared it to thumbprints on his windpipe.

Despite this hostility, Denis Brian defends interviews as a very useful medium of communication. In just a few minutes, an interview can reveal a person’s face, beliefs, and lifestyle. It can make celebrities seem ordinary and turn unknown people into stars. The interviewer’s power is huge because they can shape or damage reputations. So the interview is both valuable and dangerous: it gives insight but can also invade privacy.

Part II is an actual interview with Umberto Eco, Italian professor and author of The Name of the Rose, by Mukund Padmanabhan. When asked how he manages to write novels, essays, children’s books, and academic texts while teaching, Eco reveals his secret: interstices. He uses small gaps of time between tasks to write. Instead of wasting minutes, he writes a few pages in those spaces.

Eco says he writes novels on Sundays. The Name of the Rose, a complex novel about medieval monks, theology, and detective work, became a bestseller with 10 million copies. He did not expect such success because the book was serious and scholarly. But readers loved its detective-story style, which made tough ideas accessible. Eco clarifies that he is not mainly a novelist; he is a university professor who writes novels on weekends.

qa.q: What are some of the positive views on interviews?
qa.a: Interviews are useful and powerful means of communication. Some people consider them a source of truth and even an art.

qa.q: Why do most celebrity writers despise being interviewed?
qa.a: They feel interviews are an intrusion into their private lives. Some writers feel interviews wound them or reduce their dignity.

qa.q: What is the belief in some primitive cultures about being photographed?
qa.a: Some primitive cultures believe that taking a photograph of a person is like stealing that person’s soul.

qa.q: What do you understand by the expression “thumbprints on his windpipe”?
qa.a: It means interviews feel painful, suffocating, and oppressive to the person being interviewed.

qa.q: Who, in today’s world, is our chief source of information about personalities?
qa.a: The interviewer is our chief source of information about personalities because we learn about famous people mainly through interviews.

qa.q: Do you think Umberto Eco likes being interviewed? Give reasons for your opinion.
qa.a: Yes, Umberto Eco seems comfortable with interviews. He answers the questions clearly and freely. He explains his ideas about writing, academic work, novels, and success without irritation.

qa.q: How does Eco find the time to write so much?
qa.a: Eco uses the empty spaces in his life, which he calls “interstices.” He uses small gaps of time, like waiting for someone or travelling, to think and write.

qa.q: What was distinctive about Eco’s academic writing style?
qa.a: Eco’s academic writing had a narrative style. He did not write in a dry academic manner; he explained the journey of his research, including trials and errors.

qa.q: Did Umberto Eco consider himself a novelist first or an academic scholar?
qa.a: Eco considered himself an academic scholar first. He said he was a university professor who wrote novels on Sundays.

qa.q: What is the reason for the huge success of the novel The Name of the Rose?
qa.a: Eco says the success of The Name of the Rose is a mystery. It may have become popular because it combines a detective story with serious subjects like history, theology, and philosophy.
